summary: Crossover investors gain informational and behavioral advantages at IPO by having years of pre-IPO diligence history versus investors starting from the S-1
summary_extended: Baker argues that crossover investors hold a structural informational advantage over public-only investors at the IPO moment and in the months following it. Specifically, he contends that for any company worthy of going public, a crossover fund should already possess 3-5 years of diligence history, multiple management meetings, and a deep understanding of the business — compared to public-only investors who are starting from scratch with the S-1 prospectus. This knowledge asymmetry is most exploitable at two moments: the IPO itself, where crossover investors can price risk more accurately, and at lockup expiry, when low-information or constrained sellers (e.g., early employees, pre-IPO funds forced to sell) create artificial price dislocations that informed crossover investors can exploit as buying opportunities. The investment implication is structural and strategy-level rather than company-specific: Baker is articulating why the crossover model generates alpha, not making a call on any individual equity.
detail: Baker argues that for any company good enough to go public, a crossover investor should already have 3-5 years of history, multiple management meetings, and deep diligence — versus public-only investors starting from scratch. This creates an asymmetric knowledge advantage at IPO and during lockup expiry when low-information sellers create buying opportunities.
confidence_evidence: Baker describes this as a core structural advantage of the crossover model and references specific tactics like buying during lockup expiry.
quote_fragment: instead of starting from scratch with the S-1, you might have 3 to 5 years of history
